sections.header: Verses 1-4 — The Sword, the Razor, and the Three Thirds

heb.paragraph: Ezekiel shaves his head and beard with a sword used as a razor — a deeply humiliating act for any Israelite and especially for a priest. Levitical law prohibited priests from shaving their heads (Lev 21:5). The sign-act dramatizes the total humiliation and defilement that Jerusalem will suffer.

refs.note: Priests must not shave their heads — Ezekiel's sign-act deliberately violates his priestly identity to dramatize the depth of God's judgment.
The LORD will use a razor hired from beyond the Euphrates to shave Israel — a shared metaphor connecting the two prophets.

notes.note: Using a sword as a razor combines two images: military destruction (sword) and personal humiliation (shaving). The judgment is both violent and degrading.
Even the remnant tucked in the garment suffers further loss — a few strands are burned. Judgment reaches even the survivors; no one escapes unscathed.

Calvin reads the three portions as representing the comprehensive nature of divine judgment: it comes by multiple means and leaves almost nothing behind. The remnant tucked in the garment shows God preserving a seed even in total judgment.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The Hebrew herev ('sword') used as a razor is intentionally jarring — the instrument of war becomes the tool of personal debasement, collapsing the distinction between battle and shame.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block emphasises the priestly scandal: a Zadokite priest shaving his head violates Lev 21:5 and Ezek 44:20. God commands his own priest to break his own rules, signaling that the priestly system itself is under judgment.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli classifies this as a prophetic sign-act (Zeichenhandlung) that functions as sympathetic magic within the prophetic tradition: the enacted fate of the hair enacts the fate of the city. The prophet's body becomes a map of Jerusalem's destruction.

hist.context: The hair is divided into thirds: one-third burned in the city (plague/famine), one-third struck with the sword around the city (death by enemy), one-third scattered to the wind (exile). A few strands are tucked into the prophet's garment folds (the remnant), but even some of these are thrown into the fire. The sign-act maps Jerusalem's fate with mathematical precision.

heb.paragraph: The plural 'abominations' (to'evot) is a key word in Ezekiel, occurring 43 times. It denotes acts that are fundamentally offensive to God's holiness — idolatry, ritual violation, and covenant betrayal. Jerusalem has committed more abominations than the surrounding nations, making her judgment more severe.

refs.note: The covenant curses predicted cannibalism during siege — Ezekiel 5:10 fulfills this terrible promise.
Greater knowledge and privilege demand greater accountability — the principle underlying Jerusalem's harsher judgment compared to the nations.
Lamentations confirms the horror: compassionate women cooked their own children during the siege.

notes.note: Jerusalem placed in the center of the nations — not geographically but theologically. Israel was meant to be God's display case for covenant faithfulness.
God will do what he has never done before and will never do again — the destruction of 586 BC is unique in divine judgment history.
Cannibalism during the siege — fathers eating children and children eating fathers — represents the ultimate breakdown of covenant community.
The triple formula 'anger, wrath, rebuke' expresses the completeness of divine judgment. When God's fury is spent, they will know he has spoken in zeal.
Calvin stresses that privilege increases accountability. Israel received more light than any nation; therefore her darkness is judged more severely.
Calvin connects the detestable practices to the withdrawal of divine presence: 'I myself will withdraw' — the worst judgment is the departure of God.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase 'in the center of the nations' reflects the ancient cosmological concept of Jerusalem as the navel of the earth (cf. Ezek 38:12), a theological rather than geographical claim.
The oath formula 'as surely as I live' (hay-ani) appears 14 times in Ezekiel and nowhere else in the prophets — a distinctive Ezekielian marker of divine commitment.

block.source: Daniel I. Block, Ezekiel, NICOT (2 vols., 1997/1998) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Block reads the entire oracle as covenant lawsuit: God as suzerain charges Jerusalem with violating the treaty stipulations. The punishment (sword, famine, plague, exile) corresponds precisely to the covenant curses in Leviticus 26 and Deuteronomy 28.

zimmerli.source: Walther Zimmerli, Ezekiel, Hermeneia (2 vols., 1979/1983) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Zimmerli identifies the recognition formula ('they will know that I, the LORD, have spoken in my zeal') as the theological climax: judgment is not arbitrary but purposeful — it produces knowledge of YHWH. Even wrath serves revelation.

hist.context: The interpretation reveals the shocking theological logic: Jerusalem, placed 'in the center of the nations' (5:5) as a model of God's order, has become worse than the pagan nations around her. The covenant privilege that should have produced holiness instead produced greater accountability and more severe judgment. 'To whom much is given, much will be required' (Luke 12:48).

rows.label: vv.1-4
rows.text: Sign-Act: The Razor and the Three Thirds

rows.label: vv.13-17
rows.text: Oracle: Sword, Famine, Plague, and Wrath

lit.note: Sign-act (vv.1-4) followed by divine interpretation (vv.5-17). The hair becomes a map of Jerusalem's destruction.

themes.note: Judgment peaks at 10: the chapter is unrelenting condemnation. Covenant at 8: the curses of Lev 26 and Deut 28 are being executed. Mercy at 1: the tiny remnant in the garment folds is barely visible.

It denotes acts that are fundamentally offensive to God's holiness — idolatry, ritual violation, and covenant betrayal. Jerusalem has committed more abominations than the surrounding nations, making her judgment more severe.

trans.title: Comparing translations in Shaved Head and Divided Hair

translations.text: When the days of your siege come to an end, burn a third of the hair inside the city. Take a third and strike it with the sword all around the city. And scatter a third to the wind. For I will pursue them with drawn sword.

A third part you shall burn in the fire in the midst of the city, when the days of the siege are completed. And a third part you shall take and strike with the sword all around the city. And a third part you shall scatter to the wind, and I will unsheathe the sword after them.
Again, take a few of these and throw them into the fire and burn them up. A fire will spread from there to all Israel.
And of these again you shall take some and cast them into the midst of the fire and burn them in the fire. From there a fire will come out into all the house of Israel.

thread.text: Priests must not shave their heads — Ezekiel's sign-act deliberately violates his priestly identity to dramatize the depth of God's judgment.
The LORD will use a razor hired from beyond the Euphrates to shave Israel — a shared metaphor connecting the two prophets.
The covenant curses predicted cannibalism during siege — Ezekiel 5:10 fulfills this terrible promise.
Greater knowledge and privilege demand greater accountability — the principle underlying Jerusalem's harsher judgment compared to the nations.
Lamentations confirms the horror: compassionate women cooked their own children during the siege.

coaching.tip: Ezekiel shaves his head and divides the hair into thirds: burned, struck with a sword, scattered to the wind (vv. 1–4). A few strands are tucked into his garment — the remnant. Even from those, some are thrown into the fire. The visual is devastating: systematic destruction with diminishing hope at each stage. The sign-act is a statistical model of catastrophe expressed through a haircut.

'She has rebelled against my laws and decrees more than the nations' (v. 6). The charge isn't that Jerusalem is as bad as the pagans — it's worse. Greater revelation creates greater accountability. Israel's covenant privilege doesn't protect them; it raises the standard. Jesus echoes this principle: 'From everyone who has been given much, much will be demanded' (Luke 12:48).
